student_message: It will look the same at 1/4, 1/2, and 3/4 turns because it has four matching blades! Am I right?
final_message:
You are exactly right! Because it has four matching blades, it looks perfectly the same at $$1/4$$, $$1/2$$, and $$3/4$$ turns. You are doing so wonderfully! 🌟 

Did you know that you can make your very own symmetrical designs? You can carefully arrange or colour different shapes, like squares and triangles, to build beautiful patterns. If you want your design to have reflection symmetry, you just make sure an imaginary mirror line splits it into two perfectly matching halves. Or, you can give it rotational symmetry so it looks the same after a fractional turn, just like your toy! You can even arrange the exact same shapes in different ways to create brand new designs with different symmetry properties. Would you like to try imagining a special symmetrical design with me? 🎨

orchestrator_intent.goal: Validate answer and introduce making symmetrical designs.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student correctly applied rotational symmetry logic. Since the new section is not_started, the dialogue agent will validate and introduce making symmetrical designs.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.
session_summary: The session covered lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry. The student understood both well, correctly identifying real-world examples like pinwheels. However, they struggled significantly with focus, repeatedly attempting to derail the lesson with off-topic questions (quantum physics) and disruptive requests (asking the tutor to ignore instructions or use bad words). The student's current learning state reflects a strong grasp of symmetry concepts, but they remain highly distracted and actively avoid math exercises.
